data.source: Internet-based survey using the 10-item Kessler Psychological Distress Scale (K10)
data.wave_or_subset: University students in Jordan during May 2020
data.access_details: Data available upon request to the corresponding author
data.notes: Cross-sectional, non-probabilistic sampling; majority undergraduates (84.8%); K10 adapted by skipping Q3 and Q6 unless preceding conditions were met; K10 score range 10–50 with severity categories (10–19 none, 20–24 mild, 25–29 moderate, 30–50 severe). Ethical procedures followed (anonymity, informed consent).

method.description: A cross-sectional internet-based survey of university students in Jordan conducted in May 2020 measured psychological distress using the K10 scale. Responses were scored and categorized into severity levels. Statistical analyses included descriptive statistics, multinomial logistic regression to examine associations between distress severity, gender, and motivation for distance learning, and Spearman correlation between age and distress. Analyses were performed in SPSS v23 with significance set at P<0.05.
steps: Design an online questionnaire incorporating the K10 scale and demographic/learning motivation items.
Distribute the survey to university students in Jordan during May 2020.
Collect responses (397 total) and retain complete cases (381) for analysis.
Score K10: omit Q3 and Q6 unless their preceding conditions were met; compute total (10–50) and classify severity (10–19 none, 20–24 mild, 25–29 moderate, 30–50 severe).
Compute descriptive statistics for demographics, K10 scores, and coping behaviors.
Use multinomial logistic regression to test associations between distress severity (outcome) and predictors (gender and motivation for distance learning).
Assess correlation between age and K10 total score using Spearman’s rho.
Set statistical significance at P<0.05 and conduct all analyses in SPSS Version 23.0.
method.models: Multinomial logistic regression; Spearman rank-order correlation; descriptive statistics
method.outcome_variable: Psychological distress measured by K10 (total score and categorized severity levels)
method.independent_variables: Gender; motivation for distance learning; age (for correlation with K10 score)

results.summary: High psychological distress was observed among university students in Jordan during the COVID-19 pandemic. The mean K10 score was 34.2 (SD 9.4), with 69.5% classified as having severe distress. Females were more likely than males to experience mild and severe distress in multinomial logistic regression. There was a significant inverse relationship between motivation for distance learning and distress levels, and younger age was associated with higher distress. Over half of students reported no motivation for online learning, and social networking was the most common coping activity. Medication use was reported by 12.9% of respondents.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Odds of mild distress (male vs female)
Odds of severe distress (male vs female)
Odds of moderate distress (male vs female)
Association between motivation for distance learning and distress severity (higher motivation vs lower)
